metadata.title: Base accessibility review rubric
metadata.description: Default severity rules for /gaia-review-a11y aligned with WCAG 2.1 Level A and AA. Layer 1 of the four-layer rubric composition; Level-A keyboard and contrast failures are Critical because they block conformance entirely.

severity_rules.pattern: Interactive control is built from `<div>` or `<span>` with click handlers instead of a native `<button>`, `<a>`, `<input>`, or `<select>`.
severity_rules.description: Non-semantic interactive elements lack default keyboard support, focus styles, and correct accessibility-tree role — assistive tech cannot operate them.
severity_rules.remediation: Use the appropriate native element (`<button>` for actions, `<a href>` for navigation); only fall back to ARIA when no native element fits.

severity_rules.pattern: Heading structure skips levels (h1 → h3) or the page lacks a single `<h1>` describing its purpose.
severity_rules.description: Heading skips break the document outline that screen-reader users navigate by.
severity_rules.remediation: Maintain a continuous heading hierarchy starting at `<h1>`; use CSS — not heading level — for visual size.

severity_rules.pattern: Form input has no associated `<label>` (either wrapping or `for`/`id` linkage) and no `aria-label` / `aria-labelledby`.
severity_rules.description: Unlabeled form controls leave screen-reader users without any prompt for what to enter.
severity_rules.remediation: Associate every input with a visible `<label>`; reserve `aria-label` for genuinely label-less controls (e.g., icon-only buttons).

severity_rules.pattern: Custom widget uses an ARIA `role` (e.g., `role=button`, `role=dialog`) without the matching keyboard-interaction contract (Enter/Space activation, Escape dismissal, focus trap).
severity_rules.description: ARIA promises behaviour to assistive tech; if the underlying widget does not implement the matching keyboard contract, the role is a lie.
severity_rules.remediation: Implement the full WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices keyboard contract for the role, or use the native element instead.

severity_rules.pattern: Native semantic element is overridden with a contradicting ARIA role (e.g., `<button role=link>`, `<a role=button>`).
severity_rules.description: Conflicting roles confuse the accessibility tree and produce inconsistent assistive-tech announcements.
severity_rules.remediation: Remove the redundant or conflicting role; pick the native element whose semantics match the intended behaviour.

severity_rules.pattern: Live-region announcement (`aria-live`, `role=status`, `role=alert`) is missing for an asynchronously-updated region the user cares about (form errors, toasts).
severity_rules.description: Without a live region, screen-reader users miss state changes that visual users see.
severity_rules.remediation: Wrap the dynamic region in `aria-live=polite` (or `assertive` for blocking errors) and update its text content when state changes.

severity_rules.pattern: Interactive control is reachable only via mouse — no `tabindex`, no native focusability, or `tabindex=-1` on a primary action.
severity_rules.description: Keyboard-only users cannot operate the application — a WCAG 2.1.1 Level A failure that blocks conformance.
severity_rules.remediation: Make every interactive control focusable with native semantics; reserve `tabindex=-1` for programmatic focus targets only.

severity_rules.pattern: Focus moves into a modal, menu, or overlay but cannot escape with Escape and is not trapped — focus leaks to the underlying page.
severity_rules.description: Broken focus management for modal dialogs is a WCAG 2.1.2 Level A failure (no keyboard trap, no exit).
severity_rules.remediation: Trap focus within the modal while open; restore focus to the trigger on close; honour Escape as the dismiss key.

severity_rules.pattern: Page lacks a visible focus indicator on interactive controls (CSS `outline: none` without an alternative focus style).
severity_rules.description: Without a visible focus indicator, sighted keyboard users cannot tell which control will receive their next keystroke (WCAG 2.4.7 Level AA).
severity_rules.remediation: Provide a visible focus style (outline, box-shadow, or background change) with adequate contrast against the surrounding UI.

severity_rules.pattern: Body text has a contrast ratio below 4.5:1 against its background (Level AA threshold for text < 18pt or < 14pt bold).
severity_rules.description: Insufficient contrast makes text unreadable for low-vision users — a WCAG 1.4.3 Level AA failure that blocks legal conformance in most jurisdictions.
severity_rules.remediation: Adjust foreground or background until contrast ≥ 4.5:1; verify with a contrast checker against every theme variant.

severity_rules.pattern: Interactive UI component (buttons, form borders, focus indicators) has a contrast ratio below 3:1 against its adjacent colours.
severity_rules.description: Low-contrast UI components are invisible to many low-vision users — a WCAG 1.4.11 Level AA failure for non-text contrast.
severity_rules.remediation: Raise the contrast of borders, icons, and focus indicators to ≥ 3:1 against their surroundings.

severity_rules.pattern: Status, error, or success information is conveyed by colour alone (red/green text, coloured dot) without a complementary icon, text, or shape.
severity_rules.description: Colour-only meaning is invisible to users with colour vision deficiency — a WCAG 1.4.1 Level A failure.
severity_rules.remediation: Pair colour with a redundant cue (icon, label, shape) so the information is perceivable without colour.

severity_rules.pattern: Image or icon that conveys meaning lacks an `alt` attribute (or has empty `alt=""` despite being meaningful).
severity_rules.description: Missing alt text leaves screen-reader users without the information sighted users see — WCAG 1.1.1 Level A.
severity_rules.remediation: Provide a concise `alt` description for meaningful images; use `alt=""` only for decorative images.

severity_rules.pattern: Page provides no skip-link to the main content — keyboard and screen-reader users must traverse the navigation on every page load.
severity_rules.description: Skip-links are a WCAG 2.4.1 Level A bypass mechanism that lets users jump past repeated navigation.
severity_rules.remediation: Add a `Skip to main content` link as the first focusable element on every page.
